According to a 1992 Psychology Today article (https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/articles/199207/bright-lights-big-mystery) there significant differences between the near death experiences reported by different cultures:

"* Many of the Africans interpreted the event as somewhat evil; half thought the experience signified that they were somehow "bewitched." Another called it a "bad women."
* Among 400 Japanese NDErs, many reported seeing long, dark rivers and beautiful flowers, two common symbols that frequently appear as images in Japanese art.
* East Indians sometimes see heaven as a giant bureaucracy, and frequently report being sent back because of clerical errors!
* Americans and English say they are sent back for love or to perform a job.
* Natives of Micronesia often see heaven as similar to a large, brightly lit American city with loud, noisy cars and tall buildings."

Do you have a sense of the occult reason for these divergent cultural and regional afterlife experiences?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The notion that everyone gets the same sort of afterlife experience is far from universal in world religions. As I see it, there are two factors involved. The second is that the next world is just as complicated and diverse as this one, and different areas of the theosphere vary according to your religious involvements and the spiritual terrain of the land in which you live.

comments.title: A Strange Encounter
comments.content:
I had an interesting experience the other day: I was out for a walk and my thoughts turned to the Norse gods -- in think in relation to the virtues they exhibited -- and my thoughts wandered briefly to Loki as a kind of outlier: even when he does things with good outcomes, they're either done in a bad way or for bad reasons and the gods suffer as much as they benefit from it.

I had a sudden feeling that I needed to drop this line of thought, and I tried to get back to the other gods, but I felt a presence wanting me to acknowledge it. I got the sense that it was Loki. I told it to go away but it wouldn't. I should have invoked a holy symbol then and there but I felt that if it really was Loki then he's a god and it wouldn't do any good anyway (I now recognize that this was not exactly my thought). So I went along and soon had the thought: I can recognize the bad in the good things he does, why should I not recognize the good in the bad things he does?

As I thought about this, something still felt off. I felt hot, muggy, and a little confused, which I knew to be signs that I was dealing with a malign spirit. But hey, it was a hot and humid day. I struggled with whether or not to follow up on the thought and whether I was dealing with a god or something else.

I thought multiple times about tracing the sign of the hammer in case it was something malign but was stopped by a thought that I shouldn't do that and it wouldn't do any good. Finally I decided if this really was a god I was dealing with, he shouldn't mind me invoking a holy symbol, and so I did. The presence vanished immediately.

I'm not sure precisely what I encountered or what it means. Was it a demon pretending to be Loki, or Loki pretending to be a demon?

replies.title: Re: A Strange Encounter
replies.content: It's very hard to say, but it was certainly not a positive being. Next time it happens, draw the hammer symbol right away!

comments.title: Tired and Sad
comments.content:
Eleven weeks ago I lost a tooth I had been hoping to save. It was my third such experience in the past few years and I was bitterly disappointed. In fact, due to circumstances involving an incompetent public dental system and many weeks of ultimately pointless buildup, I was devastated.

But I did expect to recover after a while: a week or two, a few weeks at most. But that has not happened. Instead I have experienced more or less they may.

Last night online I heard a reading from a speech by Rudolf Steiner in which he mentions that a severed body part remains connected with the corresponding portion of the etheric body. And then I remembered prior discussions here and elsewhere concerning phantom limbs and their connection with the etheric body. And so I wonder: might the loss of a tooth, or indeed of several, coupled with conditions of great psychological stress, have depleted me etherically in some way? If so, what if anything can I do about it?

I have basic skills in HOGD rituals, adapted to Hellenic deities: the LBRP, circulatingq the light, invocation of the Sun, and my Hellenic answer to the Rose Cross. I believe they are sound adaptations.
I also have some acquaintance with the Tarot, of which I asked a few questions in my haphazard twenty-questions way:

Q: Am I thinking along the right lines in this matter?

A: VIII of Pentacles

Q: is there anything I can do about it?

A: V The Hierophant

Q: Is anything being done against me through my lost tooth, perhaps even magically?

A: Queen of Wands

Q: What is the best possible outcome?

A: XXI The World


My recollection of how I formulated the last question is imperfect and may be off a bit.

Should you happen to have any constructive comments or suggestions, I’d be very glad.

replies.title: Re: Tired and Sad
replies.content: In your place I'd perform the equivalent of the Rose+Cross ritual daily, and offer incense and prayers to whichever healing deity you feel closest to; the Hierophant as answer to your second question suggests that. It doesn't look as though anyone's using the tooth to get at you, for what it's worth.

replies.title: Re: Tired and Sad
replies.content:
Thank you! Will do.

My friendliest deity is Helios, both by horoscope and by personal predilection. I asked Apollon for help with this beforehand, and felt very let down by him. On the other hand, maybe something even worse might have happened as a result of the root canal I was hoping to receive. It’s not as though the gods are likely to feel obliged to explain themselves to us.

comments.title: Golden dawn
comments.content:
A few newbie Golden Dawn related questions:

1. What is the better material for a robe from a practical point of view: cotton or silk?

2. I understand that GD banishing rituals can disenchant magically charged items. How can one prevent that from happening?

3. This is more esoteric, but what actually happens when I banish or invoke an element in the LRP? If I inovke Earth does that mean e.g. I'm improving my health, or am I asking to run into more situations related to health. Because it makes a difference.

replies.title: Re: Golden dawn
replies.content:
1) Cotton. Silk blocks magical energies, and you want the energies to flow through you in your ritual work.

2) Wrap the objects in silk or linen to preserve the charge. You want to do that anyway with any consecrated item.

3) When you banish or invoke an element with the pentagram rituals you either disperse the elemental force or you draw more of it toward you. The effect on your health will depend on your current state of elemental balance -- if you're way over in an Airy direction, invoking Earth will pull you back toward balance and better health, while if you're inbalanced in an Earthy direction invoking more Earth will make it worse.

replies.title: Re: Golden dawn
replies.content:
Thank you, JMG.

Regarding 2:

The four elemental tools of the GD remain on the altar even after they are consecrated, are they not affected by the banishings?

replies.title: Re: Golden dawn
replies.content: The working tools -- Lotus Wand, Sword, Lamen, and elemental weapons -- are instruments you use to direct your own power, and the power of the Sephiroth you invoke. They're not supposed to retain a charge of their own. Study the rituals of consecration for the working tools and compare and contrast those to the rituals for consecrating talismans, and notice the differences.

replies.title: Re: Golden dawn
replies.content:
JMG:

I have a follow up question on this point. If I perform the LBRP in the same space where my Tarot cards are and the cards are not wrapped in silk, can the LBRP foul up the cards for divination? I did this a few days ago and got weird readings afterward.

replies.title: Re: Golden dawn
replies.content: Yes. Get some silk or linen and wrap your cards in those, or keep them in a different room.

replies.content: Does silk block both etheric and astral energies then? I thought it only blocked etheric? Or is it only the etheric energies that mess up divination tools?

comments.title: Occult Philosophy of Imagination, Ancestor Worship, Grab Bag Questions
comments.content:
Good evening,

My first two questions are inspired by diving back into Cosmic Doctrine after a long hiatus paired with a newly awakened religious practice. The last few are hopefully less involved. As always, I'm happy to hear from the commentariat as well.


  • From your previous writings and my tiny experience with occult philosophy, my impression is that "imagination" is the combination of two different capacities on the astral plane: perceiving things that are already there and manipulating/crafting things. My question is: is there a difference in how "real" things on the astral plane are? For example, I would imagine that a God's presence in the astral is entirely "really there", something that has been part of the shared imagination of many people for a long time (like, I don't know, Disney Princesses?) might be "somewhat real", and things that I just think up on a whim and never come back to might be totally ephemeral. Is this at all an accurate way of thinking of things?

  • Potentially related to the above: how to reconcile changing historical patterns of belief and worship with the exterior/"objective" reality of the Gods? An example from the tradition I'm most familiar with: from linguistic and comparative mythological evidence, it seems pretty likely that the Indo-Europeans worshipped a "Sky Father" who was king, used lightning as his weapon, and had some kind of role in guaranteeing right action in a social context. Later Indo-European-derived cultures have Gods that clearly derive from this Sky Father, but are also clearly differentiated from each other (Zeus/Tyr/Indra/etc). Any thoughts on such change/splitting besides that I ought to meditate on it?

  • How does ancestor worship square with reincarnation? In /The Cosmic Doctrine/, Fortune warns about messing with the recently deceased, unless it is to help them on their way: would honoring my mother count as the kind of interference that would keep her from going on to where she needs to go?

  • You've said before that Golden Dawn systems and trance work don't play nice together: what makes that the case? Also, does this apply to all ceremonial magic systems, or just GD-derived ones specifically?

  • I'm reading a book (Neolithic Shamanism by Raven Kaldera and Galina Krasskova) which talks about "centering" and "grounding" exercises. For centering, they recommend the Four Fold Breath, and for grounding, an exercise where you visualize a root growing from the energy center at your perineum down into the ground and into the core of the earth, drawing energy up and pushing it back down. 1) is the "centering" and "grounding" terminology generally useful, or idiosyncratic to this book, and 2) if it is generally useful, is the Middle Pillar exercise a "grounding" exercise?

  • In /World Full of Gods/, you mentioned the kind of universalizing Mircea Eliade and others engaged in with some skepticism - would you mind sharing your full view on Eliade and how useful his writings are from the standpoint of a believing/practicing polytheist in a particular tradition?

  • In some previous Magic Mondays, you've talked about breath exercises as "where the rubber meets the road" - are there other breath exercises compatible with the GD tradition besides the four-fold breath? If so, any advice on how to incorporate them with a daily practice of LBRP, MP, Meditation, Divination?

  • Besides The Cosmic Doctrine and The Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic, do you have a "top ~5-10 works of occult philosophy"?

  • Are there any other bloggers and/or living authors on things occult and occult-adjacent that you can recommend?



Many thanks,
Aquamarine Transcendent Goblin

replies.title: Re: Occult Philosophy of Imagination, Ancestor Worship, Grab Bag Questions
replies.content:
1) Two factors influence how "real" an astral form is. The first is how much time and effort has gone into building it up; the second is whether or not something from beyond the astral plane uses it as a vehicle. The image of a god has the reality is does because it's the vessel for a real entity from the spiritual planes, and also -- if it's a traditional image -- because worshippers have built it up with their imagination and devotion over the years.

2) You ought to meditate on it. ;-)

3) In many cultures you pray to an individual ancestor only for a certain fixed period after death -- say, thirty years -- and after that it's just "the ancestors" collectively that get prayers. That makes sense, since back the day you could count on having plenty of time out of incarnation. These days, not so much!

4) When I say "Golden Dawn" I don't mean all magical systems. Nor do I mean all trance-based systems -- the specific prohibition is against letting yourself be hypnotized. That messes with the developmen of the magical will.

5) "centering and grounding" are standard Neopagan practices. No, the Middle Pillar isn't the same thing.

6) Not when you've already asked five other questions! That would keep me busy for half an hour.

7) Er, no. Meditation is where the rubber meets the road. The fourfold breath is useful as prep for meditation. Other breathing exercises? Not so much.

8) All of my books on the subject. ;-)

9) I don't follow any occult bloggers. I mostly read books by dead people, remember? Not many of them are blogging...

I just did a chart for someone who had a T-Square of 3 planets in one sign, including its ruler; 2 planets in another sign, including its ruler; and 3 planets in a third sign, two of which were afflicted by sign placement. It was one of the stranger ones I've encountered-- but these things do happen. I have 3 planets in Virgo and 3 in Sagittarius; the ones in Virgo are all conjunct and include Mercury, the sign ruler; 2 of the planets in Sag are conjunct, including Jupiter-- and are square to the crew in Virgo.

In your case, it's clear that the Sign and House in question are the most important elements of your destiny in this life.

I've also been working on another way of looking at things, which you may find helpful. The 12 signs and 12 houses all correlate to the 12 Olympian deities of Graeco-Roman theology. In Platonic theory, each of the deities is the apex of a chain of being which includes more gods, angels and daimons, heroes and ordinary human souls, all the way down to the animals, plants and stones traditionally assigned to those deities. On this view, each of our own souls is under the rulership of one of the 12 divinities, and careful attention to the signs and houses in the astrological chart can help you determine which one it is in your case.

Do the 7 planets include the ruler of the sign or the natural ruler of the house? If not, where is the sign ruler located? Which element are they in-- the elements are assigned to the gods as well; following Sallust, Fire belongs to Hephaistos, Air to Hera, Water to Poseidon and Earth to Hestia. If you correlate all these (assigning Uranus to Athena and allowing Poseidon both an element and a planet, viz. Neptune, for now), it should show you your ruling deity, which may be helpful in determining your destiny in this life.

comments.title: Does divination reveal the truth or are we reading meaning into random patterns
comments.content:
as with a Roscharch symbol.

Thought Experiment: Two practitioners of the same "level" in the same location use the same Tarot deck and layout to answer a simple yes/no question - (say) "Is this house haunted?" . One gets a "Yes", the other "No". one of them is wrong, in which case, how reliable is the use of a divination system's answers?

Context: I've been looking into one system of magic to practice for the next decade or so. I've narrowed down to Golden Dawn, Quareia, or Bardon's IIH. The first two systems have a major divination component, and seem to advocate taking divination readings as 'true answers' to base actions on (say, "don't move in/ do/don't buy the house,do an exorcism ritual, whatever).

Are divination systems a substitute for lack of functioning astral senses in situations like the ones above? If you were clairvoyant, you could "see" if the house was haunted or not, for example.

Generally, how much should one base *action*/make major life decisions on what a divination system recommends?

Thanks in advance, as always.

replies.title: Re: Does divination reveal the truth or are we reading meaning into random patterns
replies.content:
First, I'm not sure what you're getting at with your thought experiment. You could apply the same logic to engineering or navigation, after all.

Second, divination is a tool for training the intuitive senses, but it also tends to yield accurate answers at levels well beyond chance -- anyone who's done serious work with a divination system has experienced this.

Finally, it depends on how competent of a diviner you are, of course -- and the only way to find out is to start doing readings and pay attention to your level of accuracy.

replies.title: Re: Does divination reveal the truth or are we reading meaning into random patterns
replies.content:
I'm trying to understand why people get different readings for the same question, and how both (or many) readings can all be true. For science and engineering, the instruments return (at least roughly) the same reading. E.g a compass doesn't show different directions as "North" if two people were to use it. Are you saying that both people in the thought experiment will get identical tarot (or iching or whatever) answers to the same question. The house can and cannot be haunted at the same time? Different people getting different readings for the same yes/no question with divination makes me wonder about its utility as a 'truth finding' device.

answers 2 and 3, fair enough. In particular divination system as a way to train astral(etc) senses makes sense.

replies.title: Re: Does divination reveal the truth or are we reading meaning into random patterns
replies.content: The core issue is that it's very difficult to measure the accuracy of a diviner except by testing his or her readings against events. Divination is not a machine that functions without reference to its operator -- it is always, and only, a way for individuals to tap into their own subtle perceptions. The most common cause of two people getting divergent readings is that one of them is not as good as the other.

replies.title: Re: Does divination reveal the truth or are we reading meaning into random patterns
replies.content:
A very clear answer, as I've come to expect from you.

paraphrasing and extending your key point "your initial readings / interpretations are probably not very accurate, but keep at it and you'll get better over time" is a principle I can live with (true in any art, you have to do those ten thousand reps of a punch or kick - against increasing opposition- empty air, then a bag, then a fellow student who offers resistance, all the while getting corrections from your teacher, before you can depend on it in a street fight)

Thank you again.

replies.title: Re: Does divination reveal the truth or are we reading meaning into random patterns
replies.content: Also, when you get into occult matters and transmaterial realities, you will find that the so-called "law of the excluded middle" don't always work.  In other words, one and the same house will have always been haunted wheevern one person visits it, but will never have been haunted whenever another person visits it.  If they both visit it together, the first person may experience a haunted house, the second person may not; one and the same spot in that house may strike the first person as chilling and frightening, while at the exact same time the second person experiences it as warm and comforting.  And I wouldn't completely rule out that if each of those persons had a thermometer, their readings would be 10 or 20 degrees apart for the same spot at the same time.

replies.title: Re: Does divination reveal the truth or are we reading meaning into random patterns
replies.content: In my own experience, but based only with myself, when I ask the same questions and get different cards as an answer, they are basically saying the same thing in a different way. The subtle forces that give divination meaning are no subject to the dense replicability of the material world and I think that is the reason why. But also, for the same reason, divination is not set in stone, it is more similar to weather forecasting, and some situations can change fast. For example, what if the alleged ghost ran away after the first divination?

replies.title: Re: Two Questions
replies.content:
Oh dear, unless you're worshiping an entity of rot and decay like Jormungandr, DO NOT allow your offerings to spoil! You will certainly never cause offense by keeping your altar clean and fresh, but you can cause considerable offense doing the opposite.

I remove food and drink offerings the same evening as my ritual (within the hour if it's something the cats are likely to attack). Flowers I leave until they start to fade, before they begin to drop petals. I like to leave biodegradable offerings outside when I can, if they are safe for wildlife; liquids I usually pour down the sink with a prayer I found online years ago: "Into the waters of the world, flowing to the Well of Wyrd. Wyrd flows to the Gods, and back again." However, I was once told to consume a specific offering, so you may want to do divination to check in with your Gods and see what They prefer.

replies.title: Re: Two Questions

comments.title: Etheric Currents from TV and Internet: A data point (plus some other measures)
comments.content:
Dear John Michael Greer,

Many moons ago I took careful note of your recommendations (and of the lively discussion) on this blog about interrupting etheric energies with sharp iron points. In an attempt to see if I could interrupt what I perceived to be an etheric-level "pull" on my attention to the screens, I taped an open safety pin and/or sewing needle on my router, smartphone, and laptop.

These are not my only tools for combating the pull I have often felt towards the Internet-- some of which probably needs no occult explanation. I'll get to some of those other tools in a moment.

Firstly, a data point on my use of the sharp iron points. I was hoping for less "pull" towards at the screens, however, apart from some slight overall improvement, it wasn't remarkable––at first. After some months had gone by, however, I realized that there were three blogs-- one by a marketing expert, one by a designer, and one by a psychic, which I now felt a compulsion to AVOID. Literally, it was as if a heavy iron curtain had slammed down on these blogs. I cannot even bring myself to type in their names, even here! And I cannot fathom the reason for this on non-occult level. I had checked in on these particular blogs frequently, almost daily, for some years. They had always struck me as entirely innocent and quite interesting. The marketing and design blogs often offered up practical tips which I made good use of in my business. These many moons later, the sharp iron points in place, the effect persists.

I have had some important successes in diminishing the pull to the screens, none of them occult, but I mention them here in order to provide some context for that above-mentioned data point. My strategies have been various: Above all, many years ago, I gave away my TV. (And you'd have to put a gun to my head before I'd let one back in my house.) In addition, I use the Freedom app to block various hyper-tempting websites during my work hours and various other blocks of time; and finally, I have have made good use of the ideas and tips in B.J. Fogg's TINY HABITS and Cal Newport's DIGITAL MINIMALISM (neither of which have anything to do with the occult, but I do warmly recommend them). I also have deleted, deactivated, or effectively abandoned my social media accounts.

But back to occult issues. Yet another strategy which I believe has helped diminish the pull to the screens is to play classical music 24/7 in my kitchen. I use an old iPad and keep it on Classical Swiss Radio, which is free, and ad-free as well. My intuition is that this music has helped maintain the astral and etheric cleanliness in my home.

Any comment you might offer towards explaining this weird pull to the screens I would sincerely appreciate and, in any event, I hope the data point is of some interest, and that the other methods I've mentioned may be of use to others, as they have been for me.

As ever, I sincerely thank you for hosting Magic Mondays.

Kind regards,

Polka-dot Ecstatic Goose

The internet clearly has some spooky dimensions that have not yet been well explored -- my sharp iron point experiment was (and still is) a first tentative stab, so to speak, at understanding what's going on.

replies.title: Re: plant alchemy
replies.content:
Okay, what are you trying to make? If it's a tincture -- a liquid that you'll take by adding a few drops to a glass of water -- mix the ashes into all the alcohol, macerate for a philosophical month (40 days), and then filter. If you're making a stone, just add enough to moisten, and then put it in a cool dry place to harden. Whatever book you're using for guidance should tell you which to do.

When I was doing spagyrics I did all the calcination on a hibachi on my back patio -- it did a fine job of reducing things to a grayish-white powder.

comments.title: Follow Up and Divination Anecdote
comments.content:
Not a question (as I asked plenty earlier), but a follow up to some of my questions last week, with a divination experience you and your readers might find interesting/amusing. I'm working on curbing my usual loquaciousness, but if anyone would like more detail, please let me know.

Last week, I asked about going through a magical training program "by the book", and I've been thinking about that all week. Today I decided to use divination to help.

For my daily divination, I cast a set of Elder Futhark runes. On most days, I ask "what things do I need to know about today? and pick one for Wyrd, Verdhandi, and Skuld, but today I asked "what magical practice/learning should I pursue?" and used Edred Thorsson's "Nine Worlds Casting" method from Runecaster's Handbook. The gist is that you have a cloth with a section for each of the nine worlds and you cast the runes on it and interpret the combination of rune meaning and which world it landed in.

While the reading did not hit me over the head with the right choice, it gave me some valuable insights, including that the rune indicating "ritual" right on the borderline of mental and spiritual realms, and a face-down rune on that same border indicating "analysis paralysis" - go figure!

After I had gotten what I felt I could get out of that reading without further meditation, I asked three follow-up "yes/no" questions. It's pretty typical to get ties or very close results, as you might guess. Imagine my shock when my first question ("Is Raven Kaldera a good source for me right now?") came up 13-6 "no". I took that as "not only no, but hell no".

So I ask about his sometimes co-author Galina Krasskova, and she comes up 9-9 "no answer", but with that rune for ritual on the borderline, leaning towards yes. I took this as "her stuff on prayer and ritual that you've already found helpful is good, but maybe don't dive into her works on runes or spirits just now."

Lastly, I limit myself to one more question, as I've gotten the impression that the runes (or mine, anyway) aren't fans of the "20 questions" approach. I ask "is JMG a good source for me right now?" and I not only get a convincing 9-6 yes, that same "ritual" rune drew my attention by rolling off under the desk and being a bit hard to find. I've learned to see such things as "commentary" on the casting.

So it looks like I got a pretty strong recommendation to set down some of the stuff that's been interesting me lately and focus instead on working through one (or more) of your books as written. I'll be taking a look at CGD and DMH to see which one I think would suit me better. Thanks very much for your books and your time and effort here and on the main blog.

TSW!

Écru Concupiscent Pufferfish

comments.title: Psychic vampire in the family?
comments.content:
Dear Mr Greer,

There’s been a bit of discussion here around psychic vampires. I am considering the possibility that my mother may be one and wondered what you thought.

Now, if I were to read the above sentence from someone else, I might be more than a bit sceptical. I’m wary of seizing on a novel idea in the search for an answer to deeply painful and frustrating things. Not that psychic vampirism is something I’ve never heard of, but reading MM I get the impression that such things are a lot more common, and serious, than I would have thought. A lot of it sounded familiar, so that got me thinking...

I am male, and have turned 39 this month. The principle reasons I am wondering about psychic vampirism are:

-I’ve been struggling since I was a teenager with a feeling, nay, with the blunt reality that I often cannot stand her company. This is always when she does her signature behaviour of talking over me, not listening, anticipating what I’m saying (usually wrongly) and her eyes glazing over the second I try to talk about something of interest to me, or anyone else, rather than her. And never, ever stopping talking once she gets going.

It’s difficult to express why it feels so toxic. It’s not just a matter of her being a domineering conversationalist: I can handle that, and have met others of the sort (male and female). It feels as if something rapacious and deadening is taking place under the guise of happy engaging chattyness. She stares right at me the whole time, and no matter how many obvious body and facial signals of discomfort, irritation and boredom I give off she never lets up. And I often feel horrid afterwards, flattened.

-When I was a teenager and well into early twenties I developed a habit of snapping at her incessantly. This caused me significant pain and guilt (and I know caused her pain), and I made serious efforts to stop doing it. I succeeded, but the sense of something intolerable about her has never gone away for long. Now my coping mechanisms are just to escape from her, even when my partner and I have invited her over to see her grandkids. And on the phone I very often put the receiver on a table and walk away. She doesn’t notice...

-I have had a very difficult relationship with one other member of my family – an older brother. Some of his behaviour has been, I believe, quite harmful and injurious to me when we were younger though he seems to have a sort of pristine and defensive self-image which can never perceive any of it. Nonetheless I have made serious efforts to patch things up with him just so we can get on, because we also can have a good time. I mention this because that involved looking hard at some of my own faults, and making a deliberate choice to ignore and forget the most glaring of his. I feel this shows that it’s not just me. I certainly have a lot of faults, but the sense that there’s something legitimately intolerable about my mum, that reigning in my faults won’t help, continues.

-My partner feels this way about her too. However doubt arises on the point of her different relationship with my two older brothers. The oldest can be curt with her but generally seems content with a shallow relationship and getting on with his own life. The middle brother (patched up relationship) is starry eyed about our parents in a way I think has harmed him badly. He is extremely uncomfortable with my mentioning any scepticism about the family mythology of how hard my mum had it growing up, how she’s just an old dear with self-esteem issues yada yada. That reaction of his has always increased the feeling that it’s something wrong with me, that I’m being hard-hearted to my mum. But that just does not tally somehow. I feel like she picks on me the most, as having the least confidence of my siblings (I don’t think she does the staring/talking incessantly thing with them, or they can somehow override it).
Can psychic vampires have favourite “food”, while being better behaved with others?

This is getting long, sorry. Any insights at all welcome!

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Psychic vampire in the family?
replies.content:
Psychic vampirism is a very specific phenomenon. If you spend time around a psychic vampire you don't feel tense and angry and toxic, you feel exhausted and drained, as though all the energy has gone out of you. What you've described sounds different.

Next time she does the staring thing, stare back, but don't meet her gaze. Focus on the point between her eyebrows. Do it with all your focus, and keep your face flat and unresponsive. If she's trying to do something magical, she will freak out, and quite possibly either leave or try all kinds of antics to get you to stop staring. It can be quite entertaining.

replies.title: Re: Psychic vampire in the family?
replies.content:
Hi Anon, I'm not JMG, and I'm keen to know what he'll say about this, but here's my $.02, FWIW:

I don't know if it's psychic vampirism or just insecurity and the need to control others.

You have described, to a T, my mother. She treated 3 out of 4 kids this way (my eldest sister was not susceptible, for whatever reason), and still treats my father this way: it is painful to spend time with them, even though we adore Dad, because she is always talking over him, talking for him, making decisions for him against his will, etc. Her grandchildren instinctively do not like her because she orders them around without consulting them (or us!) first and then acts all hurt if they don't jump to obey... like we did as kids, and she persists in trying to buy their affection with junk food and gifts, as though that would make up for not listening to them, constantly criticizing their behavior, and getting irritated when they continue to be what they are: children. Loud, active, rambunctious, inquisitive children. I minimize visits (they tend to disappear outside when she visits anyway), and I don't let her babysit solo: there's no self-awareness there, and she's too old to expect that to change.

That said, at forty, I have a far better relationship with her than I did at 18, when I left home and got as far away as I could, as quickly as possible. A lot of that was just... growing up, learning independence, working and supporting myself, having a family of my own. Whatever hold she had on me just isn't there anymore. Our relationship is not cordial, but neither is it angry. That was the result of hard work, over two decades, learning to set clear boundaries. And that is a dreadfully important thing to do, if you grow up with such a mother-- you've spent your whole life having your boundaries violated, and if you don't learn to set them, firmly, you will attract other boundary-violators.

We actually live in the same town now, without conflict: I don't let her talk over me or anyone else, at my house (learn to say "X was talking." with a firm tone and a direct gaze. Don't back down.), and I don't do things that she volunteers me for, or keep things that she gives me (I get rid of them as soon as she leaves, and I refuse to disclose what has happened to them: she doesn't get any explanation beyond "We didn't need that, so we passed it along" whether that was to a thrift store or the trash is none of her business. This is important, because she does not know how to give real gifts: the kind you give because you think someone might like it, and which you *let go of* once it is given. She only knows how to give emotional levers. So we strictly enforce the idea that *they are gifts* and once given, all her connection to them is severed.

When I was younger, I would never have believed that boundaries could be enforced on her without simply breaking contact forever. She doesn't respect those boundaries, but that doesn't keep me from enforcing them. Any time it gets contentious or emotionally heated, and she gets visibly mad about it... after a couple of days she pretends it didn't happen at all, to save face.

I couldn't have done any of that at 18, and I wouldn't recommend trying it before you've got some real distance and independent experience to break that pattern of dysfunctional relationship. Whether it's vampirism or not, it *is* toxic, self-centered, disrespectful, and domineering, and boy, does it make those first few years of independence difficult! But hang in there, fight the good fight, it's worth it! You can get your life back, you can have mature adult relationships with other people, and you *might* even be able to establish a reasonable adult relationship with her later. Maybe *much* later. Don't feel like you have to try that right away. At this point, I relate to her much like I would relate to a spoiled child that I'm babysitting. I don't owe her anything. I'm being nice because I know her family and I want to see my dad. She's mostly polite because she wants to see her grandkids. That's a truce I can live with. I wish it were better, but you can't change other people, or choose your family.

If we had *not* been able to reach an acceptable truce, I would have cut contact completely, changed my phone number, and left no forwarding address. I've never told her so, but I think she knows, on some level.

Best of luck building your own life! Do you mind if I pray for you?

-Sepia Oscillating Grackle

This is just a long-winded way of saying I'm no longer worried about not arriving to the SoP with a fully-formed pantheon and devotional practice. I'm sure I can develop one in time.

comments.title: SOP and Depression (and/or Anxiety)
comments.content:
If it's appropriate, I wanted to follow up on someone else's post from a previous MM, in the hopes that my experience can help them on their way.

Someone was asking about doing the SOP and Depression, and noticing that it didn't seem to be helping at all.

You brought up the pattern of rumination. I think that was spot on, and wanted to expand on that from my own experience.

I personally experience anxiety as having a mechanism somewhat related to rumination. While depressive rumination leads you to slow down, my cyclic thoughts lead me to speed up and just spin out of control.

I however have noticed some benefit in the SOP. While it doesn't stop the negative thoughts from occurring, it does seem to affect how easily they spin out of control. Over time, I've begun to trust that while I'm still plagued by these thoughts, I'm no longer required to become consumed by them.

The SOP has changed a whirlwind of emotion into just... negative thoughts to be dealt with.

comments.title: 3 Card Tarot Divination
comments.content:
For years, geomancy had been my go-to divination technique, it gave me reasonably accurate binary outcomes.

Recently though, I've felt that very often, if a situation is within my power, I often don't really need a yes-no answer as much as knowledge about the things I don't know, or that I *know* but haven't thought of in that light, the unknown unknowns or unknown knowns.

For this reason I've been trying to do more tarot divination. I find it much harder than geomancy because there is a lot more room for interpretation and contradictory interpretations at that, but sometimes it seems I can get astonishingly pertinent spreads.

I've been dating a girl for a few months now, I like her and appreciate her, she's kind, empathetic, intelligent, adventurous and more.

However, I don't have that quality of anima-projection attraction going on with her that I had when I was younger. I wonder if it's a sign of getting older, and being less likely to fall into anima-projection traps, or if I need someone else for some of my other needs. (She isn't particularly spiritual in a meditative/contemplative sense, or at least she's spiritual in a more artistic sense)

I cast a spread to ask: "If I want to engage in polygamous relationships, what do I need to know?"

I used the CBD Tarot de Marseilles with a three card spread and got:


Le Soleil
Les Amoureux
La Lune

I found these cards very striking. Right away I see myself as the man at the centre of Les Amoureux with two different scenarios pulling at me.

I still find interpretation of the whole spread a bit tough though.

Right now I'm thinking that it says that monogamy, as represented by Le Soleil, is a more open, honest situation with both of us playing in the sun. Polygamy with La Lune would be a more animalistic, darker situation with things hidden and non-obvious, something more out of my element.

What do you think of this interpretation?

replies.title: Re: 3 Card Tarot Divination
replies.content: It strikes me as sensible, but I suspect you can unpack a lot more from this reading by meditating on it.

comments.title: Noticing spirits for the first time
comments.content:
This is going to be something that might sound inconsequential but has rocked me back on my heels a bit.

Unlike many of you who post here, I have no magic background or experience to speak of. I've lurked and simply been interested in an offhand way. Something happened a few nights ago that I don't have the conceptual framework for and I'm wondering if anyone can offer some opinions.

2 nights ago, I was standing admiring 2 vases of flowers by candlelight sitting on my dresser. It's hard to describe, but I had the idea of reaching out and saying "Hello" to my guides, guardian angel, or any other unseen spirit who has seen fit to look out for my well-being. As I stood there, I felt a definite warmth spreading throughout my body and a definite sensation of a "tap" on my left shoulder.

Afterwards I laid down in bed and listened to some music on my headphones. At one point I felt my eyes open of their own volition just in time to see:

A brilliant blue-white spark maybe 6 feet over my bed. It wasn't still, it appeared just long enough to zip about a foot upwards before disappearing. Even more strangely to my mind, there was and audible "zzzzp", almost an electronic sound as it did so.

My question is this: how can we know whether or not a visitation like this is from something benevolent or malefic? How do I know that it wasn't just some negative entity trying to trick me? I've tried and I cannot get any sense of intent or personality out of what I felt and saw.

Lastly, the reason this stands out so much is that for the first time in my life I feel like I have some definitive proof that there's some kind of unseen world. I've felt off-kilter since then and honestly a bit frightened - because I feel so blind in that world, and it's been real enough for me to physically feel and see.
